does: This paper develops a method to explain attention patterns by decomposing them into constituent feature interactions and incorporates this decomposition into attribution graphs. The approach traces how queries, keys, and values interact across layers to produce attention weights, making the computation transparent through interpretable feature contributions. By systematically analyzing which features drive attention decisions, the work enables detailed explanation of model decisions at the attention mechanism level, moving beyond treating attention as a black box to understanding the underlying feature-level computations that determine where the model focuses.
connects: This work builds on a growing line of mechanistic interpretability research seeking to understand how transformers process information through their components. It extends prior work on attribution methods and attention analysis by incorporating feature interaction analysis, drawing on decomposition techniques developed in explainability literature. The integration into attribution graphs connects to existing frameworks for tracing information flow through neural networks, combining insights from attention visualization research with more rigorous feature-level analysis approaches that have shown promise in explaining other neural mechanisms.
enables: This method enables more fine-grained understanding of what makes attention mechanisms focus on particular positions or tokens, supporting efforts to reverse-engineer transformer behavior. By clarifying feature interactions underlying attention, it could facilitate debugging model failures, detecting unwanted behaviors, and validating that models use robust rather than spurious features. The approach potentially opens avenues for improving model interpretability at scale, informing mechanistic hypotheses about attention-based reasoning, and providing tools for AI safety research that requires transparent understanding of how models make decisions.
